segments.title: USS Wichita as the peak US treaty cruiser
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke presents USS Wichita as a Brooklyn-derived heavy cruiser whose treaty-era design combined Pacific range, eight-inch firepower, useful armor, and broad wartime service.
segments.body: Wichita took a strong US light-cruiser hull lineage and used it to produce a highly effective eight-inch treaty cruiser for long-range US Navy operations. The lecture ties that design to Pacific and Atlantic distance problems, carrier escort and scouting roles, unusually useful armor protection, dual-purpose gun value, and wartime service from Iceland and PQ convoys through Torch, Guadalcanal, the Philippine Sea, Leyte, Okinawa, and postwar Japan.

evidence.note: The opening frames Wichita as a Brooklyn/New Orleans hybrid shaped by US distance requirements, eight-inch firepower, carrier escort, scouting, and island-support roles.
The design section explains Wichita's displacement, armor resistance against eight-inch guns, main-gun range, and five-inch dual-purpose battery.
The service-history section follows Wichita through Iceland, Atlantic escort work, PQ convoys, Operation Torch, Guadalcanal, Rennell Island, and later Pacific operations.
The conclusion calls Wichita the best treaty heavy cruiser, emphasizing her armor, firepower, and ability to defeat other eight-inch cruisers.
